text: Carbohydrate availability for training and competition is increased by consuming carbohydrate in the hours or days prior to the session, during exercise, and during recovery between sessions.
During sustained high-intensity sports lasting approximately 1 hour, small amounts of carbohydrate or even mouth-rinsing enhance performance via central nervous system effects.
For endurance sports lasting longer than 1 hour but less than 2.5 hours, 30-60 g of carbohydrate per hour is an appropriate intake target during exercise.
For endurance events lasting more than 2.5 hours, athletes may benefit from carbohydrate intakes of up to 90 g per hour during exercise.
specific_value: up to 90 g/h for events >2.5 h

text: Products containing blends of different carbohydrate types (e.g. glucose and fructose) may maximize absorption at high ingestion rates above 60 g per hour.

Whether deliberately training with low carbohydrate availability ('train-low' strategies) enhances competitive performance in well-trained athletes remains unclear despite evidence of increased training adaptation signals.
